timeline.event: The approximate period when historians believe Homer lived and composed his epic poems, though dates range widely from the 12th to the 8th century BCE.
The period when the 'Iliad' and the 'Odyssey' are believed to have been composed in the oral tradition.
The Peisistratean Recension in Athens, where the oral epics were formally written down and standardized.
The era of the Library of Alexandria, where scholars like Aristarchus of Samothrace edited and divided the epics into the 24 books we know today.

keyAchievements.title: The Iliad
keyAchievements.description: Composed the epic poem detailing the Trojan War, focusing on the wrath of Achilles and exploring themes of heroism, glory, and fate.

keyAchievements.title: The Odyssey
keyAchievements.description: Composed the epic poem detailing the ten-year journey of Odysseus returning home after the Trojan War, focusing on themes of cunning, endurance, and home.

keyAchievements.title: Foundation of Western Literature
keyAchievements.description: His epics established the foundation for Greek culture and education, deeply influencing almost all subsequent Western literature.

faq.question: Was Homer a real person?
faq.answer: This is known as the 'Homeric Question'. Many modern scholars believe 'Homer' may not have been a single historical person, but rather a name attached to a long, collective oral tradition of bards over centuries.

faq.question: Was he really blind?
faq.answer: The tradition that Homer was a blind bard comes from ancient legends and a self-referential character (Demodocus) in the Odyssey. However, there is no historical evidence to confirm whether he was blind, or even if he existed as a single individual.

faq.question: Did the Trojan War actually happen?
faq.answer: Archaeological excavations at Troy (Hisarlik in modern Turkey) show the city was destroyed around the time of the alleged war (c. 1200 BCE). While the epic involves gods and mythological elements, it is likely based on a kernel of historical truth regarding conflicts in the region.

timeline.event: Born in Königsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad, Russia).
Appointed Professor of Logic and Metaphysics at the University of Königsberg.
Published his groundbreaking work, 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published 'Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals', introducing the categorical imperative.
Published 'Critique of Practical Reason', focusing on moral philosophy.
Published 'Critique of Judgment', focusing on aesthetics and teleology.
Died in Königsberg.

keyAchievements.title: Critique of Pure Reason
keyAchievements.description: Revolutonized philosophy by attempting to resolve the conflict between rationalism and empiricism through a 'Copernican revolution' in epistemology.

keyAchievements.title: The Categorical Imperative
keyAchievements.description: Formulated the supreme principle of morality: to act only according to a maxim that one can simultaneously will to become a universal law.

keyAchievements.title: Transcendental Idealism
keyAchievements.description: Developed the doctrine that human experience of things is based on the way our minds structure them (phenomena), while things as they are in themselves (noumena) remain unknowable.

faq.question: What did Kant mean by his 'Copernican revolution'?
faq.answer: Just as Copernicus argued that the sun, not the earth, is the center of the universe, Kant argued that instead of our knowledge conforming to objects, objects conform to our a priori forms of intuition (space and time) and categories of understanding.

faq.question: Is it true he never left his hometown?
faq.answer: Yes, it is a famous biographical fact that Kant never traveled more than 10 miles from his birthplace of Königsberg during his entire 79-year life, yet he became one of the most influential thinkers in global history.

faq.question: What is the categorical imperative in simple terms?
faq.answer: It is a moral rule stating that you should only take an action if you would be okay with everyone else in the world taking that same action in the same situation. It is essentially an ultimate standard of fairness and duty.

timeline.event: Born in Weil der Stadt, Holy Roman Empire.
Published 'Mysterium Cosmographicum', his first defense of the Copernican system.
Moved to Prague to work as an assistant to the renowned astronomer Tycho Brahe.
Succeeded Brahe as the Imperial Mathematician to Emperor Rudolf II after Brahe's death.
Published 'Astronomia Nova', containing his first two laws of planetary motion.
Published 'Harmonices Mundi', introducing his third law of planetary motion.
Died in Regensburg, Bavaria.

keyAchievements.title: Three Laws of Planetary Motion
keyAchievements.description: He discovered that planets move in elliptical orbits, sweep out equal areas in equal times, and established the mathematical relationship between a planet's orbital period and its distance from the sun.

keyAchievements.title: Defense of Heliocentrism
keyAchievements.description: He provided the crucial mathematical backing for the Copernican heliocentric model, transitioning astronomy from philosophical speculation to precise mathematical physics.

keyAchievements.title: Foundations of Modern Optics
keyAchievements.description: He made significant contributions to optics, explaining how the human eye works, how lenses function, and designing a new type of telescope.

faq.question: How did he differ from Copernicus?
faq.answer: While Copernicus placed the sun at the center of the solar system, he still believed planets moved in perfect circles. Kepler made the crucial breakthrough of proving mathematically that the orbits were actually ellipses.

faq.question: What was his relationship with Tycho Brahe?
faq.answer: It was strained but vital. Brahe was a brilliant observer but a poor theorist, while Kepler was a brilliant theorist who needed accurate data. Brahe's sudden death allowed Kepler access to his extensive observational records, which were essential for discovering his laws.

faq.question: Did Kepler believe in astrology?
faq.answer: Yes, like many astronomers of his time, Kepler cast horoscopes for royalty to earn a living. However, he sought to reform astrology, rejecting much of its superstition while seeking a physical basis for how celestial bodies might influence the Earth.

timeline.event: Born in Florence, Italy.
First met Beatrice Portinari, the woman who would become his lifelong muse, though they barely spoke.
Exiled from Florence for life due to his involvement with the White Guelphs political faction.
Began writing 'The Divine Comedy' (La Divina Commedia) while wandering in exile.
Completed 'Inferno' (Hell).
Completed 'Purgatorio' (Purgatory).
Completed 'Paradiso' (Paradise), shortly before his death.
Died in Ravenna, Italy, where he remains buried today.

keyAchievements.title: The Divine Comedy
keyAchievements.description: Authored one of the supreme works of world literature, an epic poem detailing the soul's journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Heaven.

keyAchievements.title: Father of the Italian Language
keyAchievements.description: He wrote his masterpiece in the Tuscan vernacular instead of traditional Latin, establishing it as the standard for the modern Italian language.

keyAchievements.title: Synthesis of Medieval Thought
keyAchievements.description: His work seamlessly integrates classical philosophy, Catholic theology, and contemporary politics, serving as the ultimate expression of the medieval worldview.

faq.question: Who was Beatrice?
faq.answer: Beatrice was a real woman (likely Beatrice Portinari) whom Dante met only a few times but loved deeply from afar. She died young, and Dante immortalized her in his poetry, making her his guide through Heaven in 'The Divine Comedy'.

faq.question: Why was he exiled?
faq.answer: Dante was heavily involved in Florentine politics as a member of the White Guelphs, a faction that opposed the Pope's temporal power. When the rival Black Guelphs seized power with the Pope's backing, Dante was accused of corruption and exiled on pain of death.

faq.question: Why is it called a 'Comedy' if it's about Hell?
faq.answer: In Dante's time, 'comedy' did not mean a funny story. It referred to a narrative that begins in despair or tragedy (Hell) and ends in happiness or grace (Heaven), as opposed to a 'tragedy' which ends poorly.

timeline.event: Born near present-day Melmoth, KwaZulu-Natal, as the illegitimate son of Zulu Chief Senzangakhona.
Took the Zulu throne following the death of his father, with the backing of the Mthethwa paramount chief Dingiswayo.
After Dingiswayo's death, Shaka defeated the rival Ndwandwe clan at the Battle of Gqokli Hill, establishing Zulu dominance.
His mother, Nandi, died, triggering a period of extreme and brutal mourning enforced by Shaka.
Assassinated by his half-brothers Dingane and Mhlangana at his capital, kwaDukuza.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Zulu Empire
keyAchievements.description: Transformed the small Zulu clan into a massive, militaristic empire that dominated southern Africa.

keyAchievements.title: Military Innovations
keyAchievements.description: Revolutionized regional warfare by introducing the iklwa (a short stabbing spear), the large cowhide shield, and the highly effective 'buffalo horns' formation.

keyAchievements.title: Social Integration
keyAchievements.description: Integrated conquered tribes into the Zulu nation, replacing traditional tribal loyalties with loyalty to the king and the age-grade military regiments (amabutho).

faq.question: What is the 'Mfecane'?
faq.answer: The Mfecane (The Crushing) refers to a period of widespread chaos and warfare among indigenous ethnic communities in southern Africa in the early 19th century. While Shaka's expansions contributed to this, modern historians debate the extent to which he was the primary cause versus other factors like drought and colonial pressures.

faq.question: Was Shaka as brutal as he is often portrayed?
faq.answer: He was a brilliant but ruthless leader. Much of his 'bloodthirsty' reputation was exaggerated by early European writers to justify colonialism. However, his harsh discipline and the mass executions following his mother's death demonstrate he could be extremely cruel.

faq.question: What was the 'buffalo horns' formation?
faq.answer: It was a military tactic Shaka perfected. It consisted of a strong center (the 'chest') to pin the enemy, while two flanking wings (the 'horns') encircled them, and a reserve force (the 'loins') waited behind to pursue fleeing enemies or reinforce weak spots.

timeline.event: Born in Pella, Macedonia, to King Philip II and Queen Olympias.
Tutored by the philosopher Aristotle.
Ascended to the throne of Macedonia following the assassination of his father.
Began his campaign against the Persian Empire, winning the Battle of the Granicus.
Defeated the Persian King Darius III at the Battle of Issus.
Founded the city of Alexandria in Egypt.
Decisively defeated Darius III at the Battle of Gaugamela, effectively ending the Persian Empire.
Invaded India and won the Battle of the Hydaspes, but his exhausted army mutinied and forced him to turn back.
Died in Babylon at the age of 32 from a mysterious illness.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of the Persian Empire
keyAchievements.description: He completely dismantled the vast Achaemenid Persian Empire, the superpower of the day, in a rapid series of brilliant military campaigns.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of a Vast Empire
keyAchievements.description: By the age of 30, he had created one of the largest empires in ancient history, stretching from Greece to northwestern India.

keyAchievements.title: Spread of Hellenism
keyAchievements.description: His conquests facilitated the spread of Greek culture, language, and ideas across the Middle East and Asia, initiating the Hellenistic Age.

faq.question: Did Alexander really never lose a battle?
faq.answer: Yes, according to historical records, he never lost a single battle, despite often being heavily outnumbered, earning him a reputation as one of the greatest military commanders in history.

faq.question: How did he die?
faq.answer: The exact cause of his death at age 32 is unknown. Theories range from diseases like malaria or typhoid fever to poisoning or complications from his numerous battle wounds and heavy drinking.

faq.question: What happened to his empire after he died?
faq.answer: He died without naming a clear heir. His generals (the Diadochi) immediately fought over his empire, eventually fracturing it into several Hellenistic kingdoms, such as the Ptolemaic Kingdom in Egypt and the Seleucid Empire in Asia.
